content.text: are algorithm design strategies where you sacrifice memory (space) to gain speed (time) or vice versa. The core principle:
use more storage to precompute/cache results for O(1) lookup vs. recompute for O(n) time
use minimal storage and recompute for O(1) space vs. O(n) time
. In Gen AI, these tradeoffs are critical for
Trade memory for speed or speed for memory
Why It Matters for Gen AI

# ❌ WITHOUT tradeoff: Recompute every time (slow, O(n))
# LLM generation: Generate response from scratch each query
def generate_response_slow(query):
    # ❌ O(n) generation: 2-5 seconds per query
    response = llm.generate(query)  # ❌ Slow: full computation
    return response

generate_response_slow("Explain quantum computing")  # ❌ 2-5 seconds
generate_response_slow("Explain quantum computing")  # ❌ 2-5 seconds (recompute!)

# ✅ WITH tradeoff: Cache for O(1) lookup (fast, O(1))
# LLM caching: Store responses for repeated queries
cache = {}  # ✅ Hash table for O(1) lookup

def generate_response_fast(query):
    if query in cache:  # ✅ O(1) cache check
        return cache[query]  # ✅ Instant: O(1) [web:258][web:261]
    
    # ❌ O(n) generation only for new queries
    response = llm.generate(query)  # ❌ 2-5 seconds
    cache[query] = response  # ✅ Store for future [web:258][web:261]
    return response

generate_response_fast("Explain quantum computing")  # ❌ 2-5 seconds (first time)
generate_response_fast("Explain quantum computing")  # ✅ Instant (cached!) [web:258][web:261]

# ✅ Memoization: O(1) cache vs O(n) recomputation
def fibonacci_memo(n, memo={}):
    if n in memo:  # ✅ O(1) cache check
        return memo[n]  # ✅ Instant: O(1) [web:258][web:261]
    
    if n <= 1:  # ✅ Base case
        return n
    
    # ❌ O(n) computation only if not cached
    memo[n] = fibonacci_memo(n - 1, memo) + fibonacci_memo(n - 2, memo)  # ✅ Store
    return memo[n]  # ✅ O(n) with memoization [web:258][web:261]

fibonacci_memo(50)  # ❌ First call: O(n)
fibonacci_memo(50)  # ✅ Second call: O(1) (cached!) [web:258][web:261]
# Time: O(n) → O(1) with memoization, Space: O(n) for cache [web:258][web:261]

# ✅ Lookup Table: O(1) precompute vs O(n) recalc
def precompute_squares(max_n):
    lookup = {}  # ✅ Hash table for O(1) lookup
    for i in range(max_n + 1):
        lookup[i] = i * i  # ✅ Precompute: O(n)
    return lookup

lookup = precompute_squares(1000000)  # ✅ Precompute once: O(n)

def get_square(n):
    return lookup[n]  # ✅ O(1) lookup (vs O(1) compute, but reused) [web:258][web:264]

get_square(999999)  # ✅ Instant: O(1) (vs O(1) compute, but reused) [web:258][web:264]
# Time: O(1) lookup vs O(1) compute (repeated use), Space: O(n) for table [web:258][web:264]

# ✅ Prefix Caching: Cache pre-computed tokens for faster inference
prefix_cache = {}  # ✅ Cache for O(1) prefix lookup

def llm_inference_with_prefix(prompt, max_tokens=100):
    # ✅ Step 1: Check if prefix cached
    prefix = prompt[:50]  # ✅ First 50 tokens as prefix
    if prefix in prefix_cache:  # ✅ O(1) cache check
        cached_tokens = prefix_cache[prefix]  # ✅ Instant: O(1)
        # ✅ Use cached + generate remaining
        return cached_tokens + generate_remaining(prompt, max_tokens - len(cached_tokens))
    
    # ❌ Step 2: Generate full response (slow)
    response = generate_full(prompt, max_tokens)  # ❌ 2-5 seconds
    prefix_cache[prefix] = response  # ✅ Cache for future
    return response

llm_inference_with_prefix("Quantum computing is...")  # ❌ First: 2-5s
llm_inference_with_prefix("Quantum computing is...")  # ✅ Cached: Instant [web:261]
# Time: O(1) cached vs O(n) generate, Space: O(total_tokens) for cache [web:261]

# ✅ DP Tabulation: O(n) space → O(n) time (vs O(n) space → O(2ⁿ) time)
def knapsack_dp(items, capacity):
    n = len(items)
    dp = [[0] * (capacity + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]  # ✅ O(n × capacity) space [web:258][web:261]
    
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        weight, value = items[i - 1]
        for w in range(capacity + 1):
            dp[i][w] = dp[i - 1][w]  # ✅ Don't include
            if w >= weight:
                dp[i][w] = max(dp[i][w], dp[i - 1][w - weight] + value)  # ✅ Include [web:258][web:261]
    
    return dp[n][capacity]  # ✅ O(n × capacity) time [web:258][web:261]

items = [(10, 60), (20, 100), (30, 120)]  # (weight, value)
knapsack_dp(items, 50)  # ✅ 220 (optimal)
# Time: O(n × capacity), Space: O(n × capacity) (vs O(2ⁿ) brute force) [web:258][web:261]

# ✅ Compression: Less space, more time (decompression)
import gzip

# ❌ Uncompressed: More space, less time (instant read)
data_uncompressed = "Large dataset about AI..."  # ✅ 10MB
data_uncompressed  # ✅ Instant: O(1) read [web:258]

# ✅ Compressed: Less space, more time (decompress)
data_compressed = gzip.compress(data_uncompressed)  # ✅ 1MB (10x smaller)
data_decompressed = gzip.decompress(data_compressed)  # ❌ 0.1s (decompress) [web:258]

# Time: O(1) uncompressed vs O(decompress_time) compressed, Space: 10MB vs 1MB [web:258]
O(log n) index vs O(n) scan

# ✅ Transformer: Cache attention weights for O(1) reuse (vs O(n²) compute)
attention_cache = {}  # ✅ Cache for O(1) lookup

def transformer_attention_with_cache(tokens, layer_idx):
    token_key = tuple(tokens)  # ✅ Hashable key
    
    if token_key in attention_cache and layer_idx in attention_cache[token_key]:  # ✅ O(1) check
        return attention_cache[token_key][layer_idx]  # ✅ Instant: O(1) [web:261]
    
    # ❌ O(n²) compute attention (slow)
    attention = compute_attention(tokens, layer_idx)  # ❌ 2-5s for 1000 tokens [web:261]
    
    # ✅ Cache for future
    if token_key not in attention_cache:
        attention_cache[token_key] = {}
    attention_cache[token_key][layer_idx] = attention
    
    return attention

transformer_attention_with_cache(token_ids, layer_idx=0)  # ❌ First: O(n²)
transformer_attention_with_cache(token_ids, layer_idx=0)  # ✅ Cached: O(1) [web:261]
# Time: O(1) cached vs O(n²) compute, Space: O(total_tokens × layers) [web:261]

# ✅ Quantization: Smaller model (less space) vs. dequantization (more time)
# FP32: 4 bytes per parameter → INT8: 1 byte per parameter (4x smaller) [web:261]

# ❌ Unquantized: More space, less time (fast inference)
model_fp32 = load_model("large_llm", dtype="fp32")  # ✅ 10GB (4 bytes per param)
output_fp32 = model_fp32.generate(prompt)  # ✅ Fast: O(1) dequant [web:261]

# ✅ Quantized: Less space, more time (dequantization overhead)
model_int8 = load_model("large_llm", dtype="int8")  # ✅ 2.5GB (1 byte per param) [web:261]
output_int8 = model_int8.generate(prompt)  # ❌ Slower: O(dequant_time) [web:261]

# Time: O(1) FP32 vs O(dequant) INT8, Space: 10GB vs 2.5GB (4x smaller) [web:261]

# ✅ LLM Caching: O(1) cache vs O(n) generation (repeated queries)
response_cache = {}  # ✅ Hash table for O(1) lookup

def llm_generation_with_cache(prompt):
    if prompt in response_cache:  # ✅ O(1) cache check
        return response_cache[prompt]  # ✅ Instant: O(1) [web:258][web:261]
    
    # ❌ O(n) generation (slow)
    response = llm.generate(prompt)  # ❌ 2-5 seconds
    response_cache[prompt] = response  # ✅ Cache for future [web:258][web:261]
    return response

llm_generation_with_cache("What is AI?")  # ❌ First: 2-5s
llm_generation_with_cache("What is AI?")  # ✅ Cached: Instant [web:258][web:261]
# Time: O(1) cached vs O(n) generate, Space: O(total_responses) [web:258][web:261]

# ✅ Prefix-Tuning: Pre-compute prefix embeddings for faster inference
prefix_embeddings = {}  # ✅ Cache for O(1) prefix lookup

def llm_prefix_tuning(prompt, prefix_id="common_prefix"):
    if prefix_id in prefix_embeddings:  # ✅ O(1) cache check
        prefix_emb = prefix_embeddings[prefix_id]  # ✅ Instant: O(1)
    else:
        prefix_emb = compute_prefix_embedding(prefix_id)  # ❌ O(n) compute
        prefix_embeddings[prefix_id] = prefix_emb  # ✅ Cache [web:261]
    
    # ✅ Use cached prefix + generate remaining
    return generate_with_prefix(prompt, prefix_emb)

llm_prefix_tuning("Common prompt...", "common_prefix")  # ❌ First: O(n)
llm_prefix_tuning("Common prompt...", "common_prefix")  # ✅ Cached: O(1) [web:261]
# Time: O(1) cached vs O(n) compute, Space: O(prefix_embeddings) [web:261]
: Memory-limited, single query, model size constraints
Dramatic speed improvements for repeated computations:

Fibonacci(50) computed 1000 times without memoization: O(2^50) x 1000 calls. With memoization: O(50) first call, O(1) for all subsequent. For computations called N times, the speedup factor can be exponential.
Converts intractable problems to tractable ones:

Dynamic programming converts exponential-time brute-force algorithms to polynomial-time solutions. The 0/1 knapsack problem is O(2^n) by exhaustive search and O(n x W) with DP, a transformation from infeasible to practical for large n.

Without KV caching in transformers, generating each new token requires re-computing attention over all previous tokens. With caching, each new token generation is O(n) instead of O(n^2), enabling interactive-speed LLM responses.

Semantic caching of LLM responses reduces API costs proportional to cache hit rate. A 50% cache hit rate halves inference costs. For systems with repetitive query patterns (FAQ bots, document Q&A), hit rates of 70-90% are achievable.

Every time/space tradeoff involves concrete costs on both sides of the exchange. Memory is finite and expensive; time is bounded by deadlines. Choosing the wrong direction can cause out-of-memory crashes or unacceptable latency, so tradeoffs must be analyzed quantitatively before deployment.
Unbounded growth causes OOM; requires eviction policy (LRU)
O(n x layers x d_head) per sequence
GPU OOM at long context lengths; limits batch size
Can exceed RAM for large n, m; consider rolling array optimization
Stale embeddings if source data changes; requires reindexing

A critical insight: caching is only profitable when the expected future reuse of a cached result is greater than the storage and lookup overhead. If queries are unique (never repeated), a cache adds only overhead. The break-even point is when the probability of cache hit P_hit satisfies: P_hit x T_compute > T_cache_overhead + (cache_size / available_memory) x T_eviction.

The time/space tradeoff can be formalized through the lens of computation vs. storage. For any computable function f(x), we have two strategies: (1) compute f(x) fresh every time it is needed, cost O(T_f) time, O(1) space; (2) precompute f(x) for all x in domain D and store the results, cost O(|D| x T_f) preprocessing time, O(|D|) space, O(1) query time. The benefit of strategy 2 over strategy 1 is realized when f is queried Q times on values from D: total cost drops from O(Q x T_f) to O(|D| x T_f + Q x T_lookup).

For transformer KV caching specifically, the algebra works as follows. Without caching, generating a T-token sequence requires computing attention for positions 1, 2, ..., T, with attention at position t costing O(t x d) for each of L layers. Total cost: O(L x d x T^2). With KV caching, each new token only attends to the cached keys and values: O(L x d x T) total. For a 1000-token sequence, caching provides a 1000x reduction in attention computation.

import time
from functools import lru_cache

# Formal demonstration: memoization break-even analysis
def expensive_computation(n):
    # Simulate expensive computation: O(n) time
    result = 0
    for i in range(n):
        result += i * i
    return result

# Without memoization: O(Q x n) total
def measure_no_cache(n, Q):
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(Q):
        expensive_computation(n)
    return time.perf_counter() - start

# With memoization: O(n) preprocessing + O(Q) lookups
@lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def expensive_memoized(n):
    return expensive_computation(n)

def measure_with_cache(n, Q):
    expensive_memoized.cache_clear()
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(Q):
        expensive_memoized(n)
    return time.perf_counter() - start

# Break-even analysis: at what Q does caching pay off?
n = 10_000
print(f'n={n}, measuring break-even Q:')
for Q in [1, 2, 5, 10, 100]:
    t_no_cache = measure_no_cache(n, Q)
    t_cached = measure_with_cache(n, Q)
    ratio = t_no_cache / t_cached
    verdict = 'PROFITABLE' if ratio > 1 else 'NOT YET'
    print(f'  Q={Q:3d}: no-cache={t_no_cache:.4f}s  cached={t_cached:.4f}s  ratio={ratio:.1f}x  [{verdict}]')

# KV cache memory usage for GPT-style transformers
def kv_cache_memory_bytes(seq_len, n_layers, n_heads, d_head, batch_size=1, dtype_bytes=2):
    # 2x factor: keys AND values both stored
    # shape per tensor: (batch, n_layers, n_heads, seq_len, d_head)
    kv_elements = 2 * batch_size * n_layers * n_heads * seq_len * d_head
    return kv_elements * dtype_bytes

# GPT-3 (175B params): 96 layers, 96 heads, d_head=128
gpt3_kv = kv_cache_memory_bytes(
    seq_len=2048, n_layers=96, n_heads=96, d_head=128,
    batch_size=1, dtype_bytes=2
)
print(f'GPT-3 KV cache (2048 tokens): {gpt3_kv / 1e9:.2f} GB')  # ~9.5 GB

# LLaMA-2-7B: 32 layers, 32 heads, d_head=128
llama2_kv = kv_cache_memory_bytes(
    seq_len=4096, n_layers=32, n_heads=32, d_head=128,
    batch_size=32, dtype_bytes=2
)
print(f'LLaMA-2-7B KV cache (4096 tokens, batch=32): {llama2_kv / 1e9:.2f} GB')
# This is why batch size is limited during LLM inference!

# Time savings from KV cache
def attention_ops_without_cache(T, L, d):
    # Total attention ops generating T tokens without KV cache
    # At each position t, compute attention over t previous tokens: O(t * d)
    return L * d * sum(range(1, T + 1))  # = L * d * T*(T+1)/2

def attention_ops_with_cache(T, L, d):
    # Total attention ops generating T tokens WITH KV cache
    # At each position, compute attention over all T tokens once: O(T * d)
    return L * d * T

T, L, d = 1000, 32, 128
no_cache_ops = attention_ops_without_cache(T, L, d)
cache_ops = attention_ops_with_cache(T, L, d)
print(f'Attention ops for {T} tokens, {L} layers, d={d}:')
print(f'  Without cache: {no_cache_ops:,}')
print(f'  With cache:    {cache_ops:,}')
print(f'  Speedup: {no_cache_ops / cache_ops:.0f}x')
Real-World Example: LLM Inference with Two-Level Caching

A customer service chatbot serves 10,000 users per day. Analysis of query logs shows that 35% of queries are repeats or semantically equivalent. Two caching layers are deployed: an exact match cache (Redis) that catches identical repeated queries at ~15% hit rate, and a semantic cache (embedding similarity) that catches semantically equivalent queries at ~20% of remaining queries. The KV cache within each LLM call applies to every query that reaches the model, providing ~8x speedup for 512-token contexts vs. recomputing attention from scratch.

import hashlib
import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

class TwoLevelCache:
    # Two-level caching: exact match + semantic similarity

    def __init__(self, semantic_threshold=0.95, max_exact_size=10_000):
        self.exact_cache = {}           # str -> response
        self.semantic_keys = []         # list of (embedding, response)
        self.threshold = semantic_threshold
        self.max_exact_size = max_exact_size
        self.encoder = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
        self.total_queries = 0
        self.exact_hits = 0
        self.semantic_hits = 0

    def _hash(self, text):
        return hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()

    def get(self, prompt):
        self.total_queries += 1
        key = self._hash(prompt)
        if key in self.exact_cache:
            self.exact_hits += 1
            return self.exact_cache[key], 'exact'
        if self.semantic_keys:
            emb = self.encoder.encode([prompt], normalize_embeddings=True)[0]
            stored_embs = np.array([k for k, _ in self.semantic_keys])
            sims = stored_embs @ emb
            best_idx = sims.argmax()
            if sims[best_idx] >= self.threshold:
                self.semantic_hits += 1
                return self.semantic_keys[best_idx][1], 'semantic'
        return None, 'miss'

    def put(self, prompt, response):
        key = self._hash(prompt)
        if len(self.exact_cache) >= self.max_exact_size:
            oldest = next(iter(self.exact_cache))
            del self.exact_cache[oldest]
        self.exact_cache[key] = response
        emb = self.encoder.encode([prompt], normalize_embeddings=True)[0]
        self.semantic_keys.append((emb, response))

    def hit_rate(self):
        if self.total_queries == 0:
            return 0
        return (self.exact_hits + self.semantic_hits) / self.total_queries

cache = TwoLevelCache(semantic_threshold=0.95)

queries = [
    ('How do I reset my password?', 'Go to Settings > Security > Reset Password.'),
    ('I forgot my password', None),
    ('What are your business hours?', 'We are open 9am-5pm Monday-Friday.'),
    ('When are you open?', None),
    ('How to change password?', None),
]

for prompt, expected_response in queries:
    response, cache_type = cache.get(prompt)
    if response is None:
        response = expected_response or 'LLM response'
        cache.put(prompt, response)
        print(f'MISS  | {prompt[:40]}: called LLM')
    else:
        print(f'{cache_type.upper():8} | {prompt[:40]}: returned cached response')

print(f'Overall hit rate: {cache.hit_rate():.0%}')
Caching results that depend on mutable state.
If f(x) depends on a global variable that changes over time, cached values become stale. Always include all dependencies in the cache key, or use cache invalidation when state changes.
Using default mutable arguments as memo dict in Python.

The classic fibonacci_memo(n, memo={}) pattern creates a shared dict across all calls. This works for caching but causes confusing bugs if memo is passed explicitly. Prefer functools.lru_cache(maxsize=None) instead.
Building a DP table larger than needed.

For problems like longest common subsequence, the standard O(n x m) DP table can be reduced to O(min(n, m)) using rolling array optimization -- only two rows are needed at a time. Missing this optimization causes OOM errors for large inputs.
Ignoring cache eviction in LLM serving.

KV caches grow linearly with sequence length and batch size. Without eviction (paged attention, sliding window), GPU memory runs out for long sequences. vLLM's paged attention solves this by treating KV cache like virtual memory with eviction.
Assuming O(1) hash lookup is free.

Python dict lookup is O(1) amortized but has significant constant factors: hashing a long string key, pointer indirection, collision resolution. For very tight inner loops, array indexing (O(1) with no overhead) outperforms dict lookups by 5-10x.
Only cache results that are both expensive to compute and likely to be reused. Caching fast computations adds overhead without benefit. Use cProfile or line_profiler to identify actual bottlenecks.

Use functools.lru_cache(maxsize=1024) instead of maxsize=None for unbounded caches in long-running services. Set limits based on available memory: cache_size_limit = available_memory_bytes / entry_size_bytes x 0.1 (use at most 10% of memory for cache).
Prefer iterative DP over recursive memoization for production.
Recursive memoization risks hitting Python's recursion limit (default 1000). Iterative tabulation (bottom-up DP) avoids this and is typically faster due to lower function call overhead.
Many DP problems only need the previous row/column of the table. Reducing O(n x m) to O(min(n, m)) space can be the difference between fitting in RAM and OOM.
For LLM inference, enable prefix caching at the engine level.

vLLM, TGI, and SGLang all support prefix KV caching. Shared system prompts or document contexts that appear in every request can be cached once across all requests, dramatically reducing per-request compute for the common prefix.

The performance impact of time/space tradeoffs scales non-linearly. Memoizing a function that is O(2^n) without caching and called Q times with overlapping inputs reduces total cost from O(Q x 2^n) to O(n x n) -- switching from exponential to polynomial complexity. This is not a constant-factor speedup but a complexity class change.
Python built-in memoization decorator. cache() is lru_cache(maxsize=None) -- unlimited cache. lru_cache(maxsize=N) keeps the N most recently used results and evicts older ones.
Production distributed cache. Supports TTL-based expiration, LRU eviction policies, and atomic operations. The standard choice for LLM response caching in multi-server deployments.
Open-source LLM inference engine that implements virtual memory management for KV caches. Enables serving multiple sequences efficiently by treating KV cache blocks as pages that can be swapped.
Semantic cache library specifically for LLM APIs. Stores (embedding, response) pairs and serves cached responses for semantically similar queries, reducing both latency and API costs.
Memory-mapped arrays for large precomputed lookup tables. The array lives on disk and pages are loaded on demand -- getting O(1) lookup without requiring the full table in RAM.

from functools import lru_cache, cache
import sys

# functools.lru_cache for memoization with size limit
@lru_cache(maxsize=128)
def expensive(n):
    # Cached function: last 128 unique n values cached
    return sum(i**2 for i in range(n))

# functools.cache for unlimited memoization (Python 3.9+)
@cache
def fibonacci(n):
    if n < 2:
        return n
    return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2)

print(fibonacci(100))   # instant, even for large n
print(fibonacci.cache_info())   # hits, misses, maxsize, currsize

# Space-optimized Fibonacci: O(1) space instead of O(n) memo
def fibonacci_space_opt(n):
    # O(n) time, O(1) space -- no memoization needed
    if n < 2:
        return n
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n - 1):
        a, b = b, a + b
    return b

# When to use each:
# fibonacci_space_opt: called once or rarely (no repeated subproblems across calls)
# @cache fibonacci: called many times with overlapping arguments

# numpy.memmap: large precomputed table that doesn't fit in RAM
import numpy as np
import tempfile, os

n = 100_000
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False, suffix='.npy') as f:
    fname = f.name

table = np.memmap(fname, dtype='float64', mode='w+', shape=(n,))
table[:] = np.cumsum(np.random.randn(n))   # precompute
table.flush()

# Later: O(1) lookup without loading full table into RAM
loaded = np.memmap(fname, dtype='float64', mode='r', shape=(n,))
val = loaded[99_999]   # pages in only the needed block from disk
print(f'Precomputed value at index 99999: {val:.6f}')
os.unlink(fname)

Caches introduce new attack surfaces. Cache poisoning attacks inject malicious results into a cache so that legitimate users receive the malicious response. For LLM semantic caches, an attacker can craft a query that is semantically similar (high cosine similarity) to a common benign query but receives a crafted response that the cache stores and serves to subsequent users.

Side-channel attacks on cache state can reveal private information. If an attacker can measure whether a particular computation was served from cache (by observing latency: cached responses are fast, uncached are slow), they can infer whether other users made the same query -- a timing side-channel that leaks query patterns even without seeing the actual queries.

Never share LLM response caches across users with different authorization levels. A cached response generated for an admin user (with access to sensitive data) could be served to an unprivileged user if cache keys do not include authorization scope.

Set TTLs appropriate to data freshness requirements. Stale cached responses for time-sensitive information (prices, inventory, medical guidelines) can be as harmful as wrong answers. Implement explicit cache invalidation when underlying data changes.

Always specify maxsize for in-process caches in production services. Unbounded caches in long-running processes grow until OOM kills the service. Monitor cache hit rate and current size as production metrics.
: Transformer attention caching, LLM response caching, prefix caching, model quantization
: Choose based on constraints (memory vs. speed)
Since you're a full-stack engineer working on Gen AI, time/space tradeoffs are essential for optimizing
(caching attention weights for O(1) reuse), implementing
(O(1) cache vs O(n) generation for repeated queries), and applying
(smaller INT8 models vs. faster FP32 models). Master caching strategies for repeated queries and compression for memory-limited environments.
